title: 2026/02/25 GA Subcommittee A [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2026-02-25 - Governance and Administration Committee - Emergency Management Bill [Subcommittee A, Part 2]
description: 25 February 2026 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee A Emergency Management Bill (No 2) Engineering New Zealand - Richard Templer, Chief Executive - Tania Williams, General Manager Member Services - Paul Campbell, National Technical Leader Building Structures, WSP New Zealand Animal Law Association - Dr Marcelo Rodriguez Ferrere, President Whatunga Tuao Volunteer Central - Kate Aplin, Tumu Whakahaere | Manager Waimakariri District Council - Mayor Dan Gordon - Elena Gessler, Manager of Emergency Management Kay Jones Amateur Radio Emergency Communications - John Murphy, CEO Te Runanganui o Ngati Porou - Keita Kohere, Chief Adviser Canterbury CDEM Group and Canterbury Mayoral Forum - Councillor John Sunckell, Deputy Chair of the Canterbury CDEM Group Joint Committee - James Thompson, Group Manager
descriptionSummary: Emergency Management Bill. Emergency Management Bill (No 2). Engineering New Zealand

chunks.text: Welcome back everyone, and thanks for joining us. Good morning, Mike. Uh, we've got Richard, uh, ley here from Engineering New Zealand, who is our next submitter. Thank you, uh, for your patience, Richard. And, uh, we've got a copy of your written submission already, and 10 minutes to hear from you this morning. Over to you.

Apologies, I'll just get rid of the, uh, background image. There we go. Perfect. We can now. So kilda, I'm Richard Temp. I'm the Chief Executive of Hoe Engineering New Zealand. And with me is, Ah, hi, I'm Tanya Williams, and I'm the Chief engineer here at Engineering New Zealand and the general manager for membership.

Um, my name's Paul Campbell. Um, I work for a firm called WSP. I'm the leader of the, um, building structures and an engineer in New Zealand. Member, Uh, engineering New Zealand has 23,000, uh, members, and we represent the entire engineering profession.

We also regulate the profession. We ha do that under the Chartered Professional Engineers Act. Uh, and we accredit all tertiary, uh, train training for the profession, so both at Polytechnics and universities. So thank you very much for the chance to present.

Um, we support the overall intent of the emergency management bill. Number two, to clarify roles lift national consistency and ensure the right powers and protections are available before, during, and after emergencies. The key things we would like to highlight for the committee are the critical role of engineers in emergency management and our desire to ensure this is protected and recognized in the system.

The bills focus on response and recovery rather than reduction in readiness and the definitions of critical infrastructure. Hello. So I'm gonna talk, uh, specifically about the engineers critical role. Uh, it is absolutely critical that engineers are involved in both the reduction and readiness phases of emergency management, as well as the response and recovery phases through the emergency management committees.

Uh, the things that we would ask you to consider, uh, and support, uh, the inclusion of guidelines and rules around clause 2 0 2. Now, this is the clause, uh, about the civil liability protections and to clarify, uh, not just for engineers, but other professionals, uh, when they're acting under acting under direction.

So it is really important to us, uh, with rapid building assessments and with other critical functions that engineers perform, especially in the media aftermath and an event that they are covered by that civil liability protection very clearly. The other bit that we'd like there raise, uh, to the committee's attention is the ability to set up contracts for professionals before events.

Uh, and this is to ensure that in a, when a state of emergency, whether local or national is, uh, declared that the terms and conditions are already agreed, trying to contract any professionals, uh, in the immediate aftermath just adds to delay and confusion. We want things to be as predictable, uh, and lack of surprise in things as we can, which moves, uh, me on to the next parts that we wanted to focus on, which is all about the response and recovery.

So the bill does strengthen the response and short-term recovery, but readiness is, and those two points that I made before is what we are recommending. We really feel, um, that it's important that we put time in before the events, which are inevitable now.

And in that, um, we embed the, the readiness deliverables in the regional planning standards and in the national plans that will require transparent reporting on readiness for, uh, you know, the local authorities and all the agencies and into the national strategy.

And we embed pre-event arrangements so that we can mobilize faster and faster fearer and more consistently in events. So it's, uh, less surprise, as I say, uh, often cited as a rule of thumb that for every dollar you spend, uh, in readiness, uh, that saved, avoids losing, uh, $4 after the event.

And I truly have seen that in practice. And we just feel that the lack of focus on readiness, uh, limits, you know, that the bill will not achieve all the outcomes that you want to out as outlined under clause three. Um, as part of the readiness, we think that some of the definitions of critical infrastructure you should, um, review and consider.

Um, it's our very firm belief that those, um, critical infrastructure items that are needed to just maintain operational continuity throughout the event, um, need to be, um, more specifically addressed. And specifically there's a concern that hospitals aren't on the list.

It's our strong recommendation. They should remain on the list. Um, as we face more and more with the events because of climate change, et cetera, we think that, um, flood protections, stock banks, dams, things like that need to have, um, more prominence in the, um, plans.

Um, there's always issues around, um, finance and, um, payments, post events. Sometimes it's as mundane as, you know, if, if there's a big earthquake or something actually physically being able to get cash and things like that. So we think that that needs to be considered. Um, unfortunately these vents create a lot of waste.

Um, you know, um, earthquakes, et cetera, we need to get rid of things, um, stock banks slash um, debris, et cetera. So we need to be a bit more consideration. We believe in terms of where solid waste is gonna go. Um, critical private road networks also, um, we think need a little bit more consideration.

Um, and non council, um, drinking water supplies. Um, currently with all of these flooding events, there's a lot of water supplies that are, um, interrupted that are not council owned. Um, be they private bores, whatever. Um, and whilst it's a little bit grim, um, mortuaries and, um, forensic pathology, um, you know, we've traditionally relied on, um, the Army c Upfield hospitals and things like that, but we believe that there could be some more, um, preparedness, um, done in that.

Um, and a lot of these events, we think, um, traditionally the civil defense emergencies have been on big catastrophic things, but as we see more and more flood type events, we believe that there needs to be a little bit more emphasis in that space. Thank you so much. Is, is that now, Now a chance for you to ask us any Questions? Fantastic. I, I can see some questions from members.
I'll start with Melissa Lee. Thank you.

Unmute. Might be helpful. Thank you so much. Uh, I'm just wanting to, um, dig into that, um, cost that you actually talked about a dollar spent, um, for preparations worth $4 after. Could you actually elaborate a little bit on that in terms of real experience? Yeah, look, um, things like if stock banks are maintained or we, um, make them a little bit higher, for example, in a flooding event, and we don't actually breach the stock bank and flood, you know, the, the cost benefit is just enormous.

Um, if, um, what's another example? I mean, there's been a lot of, um, noise around, um, slash but you know, when we've got debris flows, taking out bridges and things like that, if we can avoid that happening to start with, um, the cost benefit is huge.

So really in the preparedness space, it's about what can we do to mitigate the extent of damage after the fact? I mean, we can never, um, totally get rid of what mother nature is gonna throw at us, but we can be better prepared and reduce the damage.

And by reducing the damage, um, we get communities back and thriving again sooner. Um, and it's really hard to put dollar figures on that, but you know, it, it's, the sooner you get the community functioning again, um, the better off everybody is. And that starts with how can we reduce the impact of the event Supplementary on that supplementary.

Yep. Um, supplementary on that question, in terms of the two examples that you've actually given, whose responsibility do you actually think that is? You know, one is the stock bank and the other one is actually the slash is it the government's responsibility to be prepared for those? I'll, I'll field that one.

Uh, having worked in local government, it's a combination of central and local government responsibilities. So for a stock bank that would typically be regional councils for slash is a combination of regional and district councils. But of course, councils only ever operate under the mandate of central government and follow the advice of central government.

So it's a combination of central and local government. Thank you. But maybe one final quick question from Mike Davidson. Tha thanks and thanks for your submission. Actually, mine kind of follows on from that is, and I think a few examples actually kind of answered it because you talk about the readiness, but I was looking at, I guess, risk reduction.

Um, and I guess by doing the stock banks, you are, that you are really talking about the risk reduction side of Absolutely. Yeah. Okay. Thanks. Thank you much. If I were you. So to give another example is a few clear, um, the built up of silt and stones and what have you under bridges on a regular basis, when you get a flood event, the bridges typically, uh, stay intact.

Um, the other, just really quickly, the other one is the actual, um, preparedness for the immediate aftermath. Things like maintaining communication, uh, routes, uh, you know, in smaller communities, often when the power goes down, the communication is out and there's no way for that community to, um, contact emergency services and things.

So there's investment in the infrastructure that we will need in the immediate aftermath as well as the infrastructure itself. Thank you so much. We have now run out of time, but I'm sure we could continue the discussion with you. Thank you so much for your written submission and also for joining us today. Tanya, Paul, thank you. Thank you, thank you, thank you so much. We now have our next submitter here, Marcelo, uh, representing the New Zealand Animal Law Association.

We've got a copy of your, uh, written submission, and we've got 10 minutes to hear from you today. So over to you. Thank you, Adam. Chair Koto. Um, it's a, it's a great privilege and pleasure to present to the committee on behalf of the New Zealand to Animal Law Association. I note that, uh, many of the submitters today, um, if not all, are going to be focused very much on the, the human side of emergency management and disaster preparation.

And so it might seem a little bit odd, uh, to focus for at least 10 minutes upon, uh, animals and non-human animals. Um, and, uh, the focus I think is important, however, uh, simply because animals are often sort of forgotten within, uh, disaster and emergency preparation. Um, and that is no better highlighted than the current, uh, legislation.

The, the Civil Defense Emergency Management Act 2002 has four scam references to animals throughout its text, uh, mostly relating to powers to clean up dead animals, um, and one very odd provision allowing, um, a controller or constable to requisition an animal in certain circumstances, but no provision whatsoever to manage, uh, animals, uh, or deal with, uh, either livestock or companion animals in emergency situations.

Um, that's despite their extreme vulnerability, uh, in any situation. And animals are some of the most vulnerable, uh, members of our society. Uh, and in particularly, uh, acute situations such as emergencies and disasters, uh, that vulnerability is increased to a remarkable level.

Uh, when we talk about sort of the, the cost of, of emergencies and disasters, uh, certainly when we see humans affected, that is rightfully, um, and occupies our mind. But what is often forgotten is that many, many animals are often sort of harmed and, and killed in, in, in disaster situations.

And, and we need to have a think about how we requisition our, make provision for, uh, animals and such, uh, acute situations, uh, that that's why we're, we're largely supportive of, of this bill, because for the first time, uh, we see clear reference to animals. Um, and in particular, uh, the requirement for regional emergency management plans to, uh, provide for the management of animals is a really welcome addition.

And we, uh, are grateful, uh, to, uh, the legislative process that is now suddenly recognizing animals as they should be. Uh, that says, uh, you have our written submission in front of you. I, I do wanna highlight two sort of key points, uh, in the current bill that we do think, uh, could be, uh, improved.

Um, and that firstly is the requirement for a, a, a national, uh, animal, uh, uh, plan. Presently, we see the ministry for primary industries provides for sort of national management and, and, and planning at, at that level, but one that only deals with sort of livestock and doesn't sort of refer to sort of the, the companion animals that are going to be, um, acutely affected in such situations.

Uh, and two, that doesn't require sort of national cohesion and, uh, engagement with regional authorities. And we think that there could be provision, uh, for a national plan of that type. Um, then much more specifically, I, I, I draw the committee's attention to clauses 129, um, and 162 of the bill, uh, both which allow for, uh, powers of entry, um, but make a very odd distinction requiring, uh, contact of an animal's owner before someone can enter a house to retrieve an animal in a vulnerable situation, uh, when no requirement is required when human life,

uh, is at stake. Now, there may be good reason as to why that distinction is made. Um, it might be the fact that animals are legally property and humans are obviously not, and so therefore you can make a distinction on that basis. Uh, but we feel that this could actually have quite a significant effect, uh, namely in a situation where there is emergency, uh, evacuations required that the burden of contacting an owner, um, or trying to attempt to make contact with an owner is simply too much in a situation where the time is of the essence.

And so we would, uh, you know, implore the committee to consider removing the provisos that are included within, uh, sub clause one, paragraph C of each of clauses 129 and 162, uh, and essentially treat them in the same way as vulnerable humans might be in a situation requiring quick evacuation.

Um, and, uh, e effective response times. Uh, the, the rest of, uh, points are sort of made clearly within, um, our submission. We, we know that the, uh, sort of international equivalence and, and oddly enough, the United States has been sort of leading on this making provision for companion animals and ensuring that the human animal bond is preserved.

But, but overall, just want to reiterate that we support the inclusion of animals, uh, for the first time in emergency management legislation. Uh, and we believe that it could be further improved. But it's a, it's a good start, and I welcome the committee's questions. Thank you so much for your submission, and I think it's, it's great to have that highlighted as well. 'cause I think one of the major points that, um, was raised, as you've noted in your written submission, was the, uh, the fact that animals weren't sufficiently re

recognized in the last version of the bill. So I'm pleased to say that you're happy with the additional, um, requirements. Uh, and I, I think we'll definitely we'll bring up that distinction that you raised with, with officials to kind of get their advice. But do you have any, um, apart from the kind of property, uh, distinction is, would there be, do, do you think there'd be any other objections to, uh, people being able to enter property just to, to rescue animals, uh, that you can think of as to why that might, um, have been drafted in that way?

I, I, I mean, I really do think, um, Madam Chair, that that distinction, that property extinction is probably what, uh, is, is, uh, requiring that because the, the provisions refer to sort of the power of preserving, of, of entry to preserve human life or to relief the suffering and the stress of people. Um, and that, that provision is simply the mitigation, um, of, uh, unreasonable or unnecessary pain of the distress of an animal.

Uh, the only distinction that I could possibly see is because in a situation where one needs to preserve human life, the, the, the, the requirement to seek permission to enter a, um, uh, premises simply shouldn't be there. Whereas we see sort of equivalent provisions within the Animal Welfare Act and general sort of common law provisions that would sort of require some sort of consent before removal of animal property is required.

It wouldn't necessarily, however, make it discordant with the general law or the general sort of, um, provisions to allow for entry and emergency circumstances and the provisions that we see in the Animal Welfare Act, which does allow sort of emergency, um, and where there are issues of necessity, uh, to allow for sort of, uh, uh, engagement of animals in that regard, regardless of owner consent, I think is important, um, to note.

Uh, but yeah, the, the, the, the property distinction is the only one, the only reason I can think of, and it's not attainable. One, given the vulnerability of animals in such a situation. And, and just with the animal welfare, um, point, so the kind of legislation that in a non-emergency situation, um, often we see organizations like SPCA might, um, intervene when there's, um, hardship.

Um, that's, and when animals are at risk that, so there isn't something already existing, um, at law that would allow, uh, those animals to be rescued. You know, despite the, the issue that you've pointed out with this, this bill, It's a very good point that you make. And presently, the only reason as to why, uh, the SVCA would have powers of entry in that situation is if they feel that the, uh, if there is reasonable belief for offending under the Animal Welfare Act now in an emergency situation that wouldn't amount

to an offense under the Animal Welfare Act, and actually the SBCA wouldn't have powers under the Animal Welfare Act to intervene in such a situation. So that's why we sort of, I, I, I'm pleased, uh, if you could put that to officials as to why that distinction is made, uh, given the vulnerability of, of animals in that situation. The distinction doesn't make a lot of sense. Yeah. Okay. Thank you so much for that.

That is really good. I think we've got one more minute left. So I'll go to Mike Davidson. Thank you, Mike. Hang, Thanks. And, and I just wanted to follow up on that. 'cause you, you'll probably know like what happens when a, like a say a dog's in a, in a kept in a car and overheat is then are public allowed to actually rescue those dogs.

Now without permission, The, the public are not. Um, and sort of, uh, I mean, because that is offending under the Animal Welfare Act would allow for sort of the entry, uh, by, uh, either a police officer or the PCA in that such a situation. Uh, but again, to emphasize, because in a, you can imagine a situation of flooding or an earthquake, there isn't offending, um, occurring under the Animal Welfare Act in such a situation.

Uh, and as a result that the powers of entry just wouldn't apply under that legislation, which, which is why, uh, this legislation would need to be amended. And it's great that there is a power of entry, as I say, but it could be aligned with the, the vulnerability of humans in such a situation. Thanks. Thank you so much. Uh, we've run out of time, but really, um, helpful points that we'll, um, will, we will put to officials.

So thank you so much for joining us today. Thank you for your time. Take care. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, I understand we now have our next submitter, Kate, uh, thank you so much, uh, for joining us today. We've got, um, the submission of your organization, which is, uh, Tuo the volunteer center, uh, so sorry, volunteer Central, which we've, uh, had the opportunity to have a look at.

And we've got 10 minutes to hear from you today. So over to you Kda. Um, thank you so much for giving us the opportunity to speak with you, uh, and speak to our submission. We are submitting on mainly around the role of volunteers and activation of volunteers in an emergency.

It's well known that, uh, volunteers are the backbone of a community, particularly when it comes to, uh, struggling with responses. Um, but also what's not so much known is there's a lot of preparation that goes into making sure that volunteers are ready to respond and be available during an activation.

Um, uh, we really do support having more engagement and visibility of local people and committees and, uh, Maori iwi in, uh, the, uh, executive committees. It's, uh, very important that those groups are involved at the grassroots rather than just, um, when a response is required.

Uh, the Volunteer Central, as well as Volunteer Centers Network, which you would've seen in our submission as a group of 17 volunteer centers around the Mortal. We live, work, and are present in the communities every day. We are working with community organizations.

We are working with Iwi, we are working with Pacifica. We are working with multicultural. We are working with former refugees. We are working with disability communities. We are working with rainbow communities, we are working with rural communities, and we do it every day. So we have the systems and the processes already in place to be able to be ready and respond and also be part of recovery.

At a national level. We'd like it recognized that volunteer centers are an integral part of a volunteer or a, a response to emergency management. And that the recognition of the local knowledge and the local relationships that are, are present within the communities are a vital part of the success of having the community ready, respond, and also recover from events.

Currently, what seems to happen is that, um, local government have put in some really good structures around emergency management, but when it actually comes to an emergency, local government staff, uh, removed from their usual positions around infrastructure, et cetera, and, um, take on roles like volunteer coordination, which really is volunteer centers, day-to-day bread and butter.

Um, so why, when a situation's under stress would you, um, bring in someone that doesn't do it on an everyday basis? And we'd like that recognized a little more in the bill that there is already existing local, regional, and national structures through volunteer centers and volunteer center network who can actually do this and do it on a day-to-day basis.

And that should be recognized when it comes to emergency management readiness, response and recovery. And we'd really like that recognized in the bill that the volunteer centers are there doing it on a day-to-day basis. So just step them up when it happens, rather than bring in someone that doesn't do it on a day-to-day basis.

Thank you for that. I appreciate the, the opportunity to speak with you, and I'm happy to take any questions that you may have. Thank you. We do have a bit of time, um, for questions if members have any. Uh, and I just wanted to acknowledge, uh, the work that volunteers do play in emergency situations. And so I just wanted to make sure that that was acknowledged.

Um, as part of our process, I know that volunteers provide, um, so much support and we just couldn't respond to emergencies without them. So thank you so much for highlighting that. I do have a question from Melissa Lee, Uh, thank you so much. I echo the chairs, uh, thanks to all of the volunteers. You talked about the 16, uh, organization, volunteer organizations around the motto.

Uh, what specific ones do you actually have in Auckland? Um, the reason I ask that is that when the Auckland anniversary floods actually happened, uh, struggled with, um, um, coordinating, uh, a lot of the response to people who had actually, uh, their homes were flooded and they had nowhere to go.

And it was actually the local school principal who actually opened one, you know, the school around my area. And effectively, you know, there were hodgepodge of volunteers. There was no organization. So I'm just trying to, um, understand how it actually all happens for your organization. So when we talk about the 16 plus fat volunteer Central, there's actually 17 volunteer centers around the country.

The only areas that aren't covered are the west coast of the South Island Rupe who, and RA at this stage, the, um, essential part of a volunteer center is to work with their local, local community and their local organizations around emergency management.

If they're not engaged in that preparation prior to an event occurring, then they're not seen as a go-to point to assist with the community response. I'm aware with the Auckland situation that, um, volunteering Auckland, which is one of the centers, uh, had made themselves available to assist in volunteer coordination.

However, the structure at that point in time did not engage with them to assist with that. And that's what we are hoping as a result of this, um, opportunity for, uh, greater local representation in emergency management committees will resolve.

So there is that understanding relationship agreement and response prior to it being required. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you so much, Kate. We really appreciate you joining us today, and thanks for all the work that you, um, and your fellow volunteers do in, in New Zealand. Thank you. Thank You, Kira. Thank you so much. We have our next, uh, submitter here now too, representing the Y Market District council.

Uh, we've got Dan, welcome. Good morning. I should also have, uh, Alan Gueler with me as well. Is that correct? We'll just check with our, here we are. It looks like we've got Eleanor joining us now. Fantastic. That's great. Fantastic. Um, we've got a copy of your written submission, Dan, just while LAN is joining, and 10 minutes to hear from you.

So you're welcome to use that time or leave some time for questions at the end. Over to you, KDA. And look. Thank you Chair, uh, Camilla, uh, Mike and Melissa. And, um, obviously whomever else is, uh, here, uh, supporting, uh, the hearing of submissions. Uh, firstly, I just wanted to acknowledge the comment that the last, um, submitter spoke of that volunteers are absolutely critical.

They, they're, uh, and last week I acknowledged, or we did as a district with, um, uh, Nima, uh, four individuals who've given 110 years of service. In fact, one individual for 50 years and the other 40. Um, so, um, between them 110, which is just amazing, we couldn't do and perform what we do without volunteers.

So I just wanted to acknowledge that. Uh, firstly, just as an introduction, um, our district by re is the fourth largest, um, territorial authority in the South Island. Uh, we're located 20 minutes north of Christchurch and sheer boundaries with the city, Selma and, uh, Nui districts.

Our district ranges from urban townships such as and CAO to the remote high country farms in the Lees Valley. 60% of our residents live in the district's four largest towns. There are six and a half thousand rural residential lifestyle blocks in the district. And I do want to come to that as part of my submission.

The district is fast growing between, uh, 96 to 2020. Our populations raced from around 33,000 to now around 74. Um, in my time as an elected member came in, in 2004, uh, 22 years, we've doubled in size and we're expected to reach, um, over a hundred thousand, uh, in the next, uh, 15 to 20 years.

So our council's position is that we support the overall intent of the emergency management bill. Number two, uh, sharing in the goal of more resilient, coordinated emergency management system. Um, we strongly support an emergency management system that uplifts the value of local knowledge critically and flexibility in both response and recovery.

We have some concerns that the bill could constrain local solutions by centralizing rule setting, and we've outlined that in our, our main submission. Whilst we agree that standardization is beneficial, local nuances must be considered. And I just wanna stress, like a lot of councils, we've got a lot of experience in this area.

We have regular events, uh, unfortunately, and we have to stand up our EOC regularly. So we, we do get this, uh, and what is, uh, having to put it into operation. And we would just like to stress that localism is absolutely critical, uh, for an effective response. We note that this bill could have significant financial implications for councils as well, particularly the time when rates caps are also being proposed.

The bill, uh, should place greater emphasis on risk, um, reduction, recognizing that early investment is most, uh, is more cost effective. Uh, over time. The regulatory impact statement notes that every dollar spent on disaster preparedness saves at least $4 in response in recovery later.

There needs to be clear co-funding arrangements that are dedicated emergency management capability grant to alleviate the pressures of fund of, uh, unfunded mandates on councils. This is where we need a really serious conversation about funding an emergency levy, like funded, like what EQC undertakes, for example, should be explored so that there is a much more, um, secure funding, uh, in place than the now basis in which it is funded.

And the regularity of events is happening all the time. So we must, we've really gotta turn our mind to that very seriously, because our councils are all facing significant costs. Like the last event we had last year was about a million dollars of unexpected unbudgeted expenditure. We just can't all carry on like that.

And then try and keep our rates at a level that we all would like to see them be. The council notes that after, um, adverse weather events, lifestyle block owners often fall between urban and, uh, urban, uh, sorry, urban and rural emergency management systems. Many are, are not eligible for MPI, um, recovery assistance, which can leave them facing significant out of cost, out of pocket costs for re fencing, stop feed, uh, in veterinary care.

We raised this issue previously, and we just ask it to be considered in designing the new emergency management system. We want to see a system that's fit for purpose, and that covers everyone. Um, and including the changes that occur in all that areas through growth and other things, which have happened over many years.

But this is a real issue that my district, and not only my district, others do face. So I just wanted to just stress that here today, we do agree with the intent of the review and the need to address past system failures, but feel there are gaps which remain unaddressed.

Most notably, the vote bill isn't clear enough about NE's role in making the emergency management system work smoothly across different agencies. It would help if NEMA had explicit responsibilities to develop national tools, systems, and the clear Roles and responsibilities framework, uh, covering the before hours to make sure coordination practice is more effective, the scale and pace of the current suite of reforms needs that councils need time to with adequate resourcing to implement changes.

Well, and we are asking for a staged, um, uh, start to these reforms, clear guidance on what is required and adequate resourcing so councils can properly assess costs, capability and compliance impacts before changes, um, take effect. In conclusion, our recommendations to the select committee, uh, are this, are these, uh, um, four, uh, several bullet points.

In principle, we support the bill with some reservations as highlighted in our written submission. We strongly support a strengthened emergency management system, but believe the system must be locally informed, adequately funded, clear in roles and responsibilities, and aligned with concurrent reforms in order to be successful.

We request that the committee do consider the impacts of not supporting local go, uh, the impacts of not considering the cost to local government with the 82.8 million required to implement the bill as per the regulatory impact statement without support, this does add significant, um, cost, which, um, we would have to absorb and again, becomes another unfunded mandate.

As I mentioned before, we each the committee to retain New Zealand's locally lead approach to emergency management by ensuring a space for local important, the development of national rules and standards. We also urge that the committee ensure development of clear guidelines so that the proposed any proposed changes can be implemented efficiently and effectively in practice.

We suggest that the committee consider the impacts of the broader reform program on the ability of this board bill to support a more cost effective approach to risk management for our communities, as opposed to funding increased costs post event. That's the point I was mentioning earlier. We thank, um, the committee for their time and the opportunity to speak today.

And between myself and Alan, I were more than happy to answer any questions. All the tough questions go down. Thank you Mayor Gordon. Really appreciate, um, the submission and the detailed recommendations as well. Uh, I just had one question you suggested in your written submission and affordability principle that allowed the sharing of costs.

And I just wanted to know, was that something that you imagined that could be incorporated into this bill? Something we, and I mentioned it again around the, um, uh, what we call the emergency fund or how we do fund these events because at the moment we're, we're aligned on subsidies potentially eroding, and maybe at a higher level if, if, if we are successful, uh, the, the, you know, the, the goodwill of the minister.

And I, I just wanna acknowledge that I do think we have a very good minister in place now. Uh, he's excellent. But, um, you know, when I say to goodwill of the minister where there are, uh, costs there, we may get a grant, uh, that might top our mayoral relief funds up to, to be directly supporting people.
